speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. And you were telling me about leaving the prison and seeing your brother outside of the prison. Can you tell me what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now I called a meeting of all the people who had worked on that [INAUDIBLE] and told them that I had not been arrested because of their organization. But at the same time I found a letter. I received a letter on the same time from [INAUDIBLE]. From the Hotel [INAUDIBLE] in Rome that things are going fine. I could breathe freely again because I was worried all the time that he might have stolen the money. We didn't know anything about him except that he had organized the first transport successfully, but there were not such amounts involved at that time. Well, we did and we was eager to organize the departure. He told us that he would be there and the ship would be there. And we need to know the Greek transit visa in order to get into Greece. And to get to Greece because also born in Greece, you need a Yugoslav transit visa, too. But we didn't have the-- you first needed the Greek transit visa which was very hard to get. The consulate said he couldn't do it. You know, I talked to an employee of the consulate. I think he was just a porter, a very low level employee. And talking with tears in his head to save the life of hundreds of people by helping us to straighten out this matter. He asked, how. I said, we'll call the people in the foreign office in Athens, and you have to ask for us because we don't speak Greek. He said he'll do this, and I promised him a large amount of money. For him it was a very large amount. He was just a porter. I remember now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I called long distance the Foreign Department of Foreign Affairs in Athens. And I said, I wanted to speak to the man with whom-- and the consulate that had spoken before. They didn't have an embassy anymore. It was a consulate now. And who had said, he can't do it. He doesn't want to get into trouble with nobody. He can't do it. And asked him through the phone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he came to the phone, I asked him. I told him-- this man-- you have to pretend now to be the consulate. And I told him what he has to say before the connection came. And he wanted to help. And he saw a lot amount of money that he could help his children. And he agreed to do it. And he said, that he has a man from the British consulate here who says that it is all right with him. They do not really object to it, as if they would object to it because of the Arabs. And this is all right if he gives him the visa. Told him now, I wrote to him in writing. He said they would write the visa, but in the meanwhile he would on the phone. He would phone from Athens to confirm it. And he phoned from Athens. Spoke to somebody who that consulate was not in. Somebody in the consulate. It was a very smart guy. And told him, that I give the visa. It was this trick that was very unlikely to succeed but we succeeded. Transit visa. Now with them to get a visa for Yugoslavia to travel over it was very easy. You got it to Yugoslavia transit visa or in mass visa not for everyone. Following in that list, they can pass through without stop to Yugoslav border and then to Athens. And then I told-- I made arrangements with the railroad at what time they would leave. And I told Erich Wolff that it's time. He should be ready. Most of the people the Betarim of the 386, I believe, were there. They are ready until at a certain time in the evening. And I went there in the evening for the departure of the train. We were accompanied by Laura and by a Gentile photographer, whom I wanted to photograph it for history.

words.text: all right if he gives him the

speaker: interviewer
text: And Laura is your wife? I just want to make sure that that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was then my wife already but unknown to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept your marriage--

speaker: interviewee
text: But known officially to her. Officially, the photographer when he heard it with his assistant through the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your marriage was kept a secret?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. It's quite a complicated story, but I don't going to let it really extend it to too much detail. And see Betar who were all joined in close order drill. They went into the wagons. And I came. Everybody was already in the wagon because it had been-- Betar had organized very well. They knew how many wagons there would be, who would be in what wagon, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came there, I fixed the problem. There was standing [NON-ENGLISH], Rajakowicz, and Dr. Langer together. And I came in. I didn't know how to greet him. Should I say, good day, or should I say, heil Hitler? Which I wouldn't have said. But this Rajakowicz, the Dr. Rajakowicz helped me. He came to me in an unbelievable way. He reached out his hand and said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Good evening, Mr. colleague, because he was a lawyer, too. It was very courageous on his part.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo was not then as sharply in control of everything that they would have done something. He was a big guy. He was a big guy. He was the son-in-law of a future possible minister of the Reich. And I decided then that if he should have trouble after the war, after Germany fell. For anything which he did with the Nazis, I'll testify on behalf of him. And all during the while with the war I was convinced that I will do that. But then when it came to it, I couldn't do it. I couldn't because he had been involved in the petition of Anne Frank. He was in charge of the Nazis or in some major position. It was Nazi civilian in the government of Poland. And I would still have testified for him, if the trial wouldn't have been in Vienna. If it would have been in Jerusalem, I would have testified for him. But I couldn't testify for him in Vienna. I couldn't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, after I had passed him, I give Erich Wolff the sign. And he shouted, in commander voice, everyone come out, in Hebrew. That everyone will come out. And the general commander, with the command, assemble according to your unit to which you belong. So it was very impressive how we in three seconds, you had to have five or six groups of Betar each with a little [NON-ENGLISH], a little flag. No Israeli flag, but a little flag. Are we are standing there. And I saw I was standing next to Eichmann to [INAUDIBLE]. How is that possible? These Jewish boys who are just the dirt off the earth regularly respond to military command in the military way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started give them now a short talk. I had told Rajakowicz before that I would give a talk in which I demand and remind them that they have to be very disciplined on the trip. And I started out with saying, you are leaving now a country where you are not wanted to a country where your brothers are expecting you with open arms. And told them, that they are going from a country where they are not wanted to a country of liberty. But I had to, very early already in the speech-- it was only a two minute speech. That they have to with absolute discipline is necessary on this trip. On the train is where [INAUDIBLE] trip. And anyone who will not be-- he break the discipline will be severely punished. And when I finished-- I hadn't planned it-- a girl, a 10-year-old girl started singing Hatikvah. And we sang Hatikvah and I, for a moment's hesitation, I fell in and sang Hatikvah, too. And this is they all stood at attention. I stood and two of the guards next to me stood at attention, too. We sang Hatikvah. Then everyone on the chain of command said, blessing to you. They already said, blessing to you and left. That was on the I think the 8th of June, 1938. In the same evening, we discovered to our horror that the pass-- Turi, who had the passports to present on the Yugoslav border has forgotten the-- not Turi. Somebody had forgotten the passports who was to pick them up. And we traveled without passports for 24 hours in the next train with all the passports and brought them to the passports.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next morning, I left for London. Before I have you go into London, I have just a couple of quick questions. Did you say it was just boys on this transport or was it boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys and girls, both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys and girls. OK. Because I thought you had said they were boys, but then you said a girl had started to sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys and girls. And how old are you at this point?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 32 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. 31. 31.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a little while ago, you use the term [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or free immigration--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Palestine. History books refers using the term illegal--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Immigration. Can you explain your use of terms, and why it was important to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used in the book the word, illegal, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. In history books, not in your books. In history books, it's often referred to as illegal immigration to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We took the stand, the attitude that every Jew has the in-born right to return to his homeland. We spoke of repatriation, not of immigration among us. You are being repatriated. And so everybody was a legal immigrant. And we did not recognize the British immigration laws because they were an illegal occupier of our homeland. And the colonial power-- you know the Jews were the first one to chase the colonial power out. With all the African nations they all followed later. And we chased out of Palestine the most important, most powerful of the colonial nations, the British empire. And surely, we took on the British empire.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were now-- you said you were on your way to London.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on my way to?

speaker: interviewer
text: London.

speaker: interviewee
text: London, yes. How did I get a visa to London?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I want to ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got a visa through-- I always forget his name. I give it to you already. This Quaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: It started with a G. OK. It'll come to us later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It will come to my mind, too. He intervened in the British embassy to get me the visa. And I got a visa. He didn't know what they are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of visa was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A visitor's visa. And I got a permission to leave. Also in my passport it was written with the right to return, which was very unusual. Because everyone has to say that he would never come back. Nobody wanted to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left, did anybody in Vienna try and hold you back? Did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't. Did they require that you give them any guarantees that you would come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see, Eichmann did not-- I think he never knew what actually happened in Berlin. Because the day after I returned from Berlin, or two days later, our office was raided in Vienna. Arrested some lawyers office of Dr. William Perl, where people are standing on the staircase to come up. And then outside was raided because some good neighbor had reported the Jews are plotting something there. They are assembling in groups at Number 14 [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the address. And the police came. And when the police came, I said, you are preparing the immigration. Here you have the permission from Berlin. And I had the permission in my hands to exchange the money. I hadn't exchanged it, the money. I didn't want to have the money around until for the last moment. I still had everything in marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the police left again. And from there, Eichmann must have heard that I had a permission from Berlin. He might have later been informed by some communication from Berlin. But at that time, it was already up in higher circles decided.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a debate later between the SS and the Foreign Office. Between the security officers and whether they should agree to it. And the SS was for it, to get the Jews out. The Foreign Office was against it because it would cause unfair with Britain. It's not diplomatic and so on. But fortunately, the SS prevailed in this endeavor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left for England--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Gestapo in any way try to make sure that you would come back or that they would still have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had to give a pound hostage. But he was not taking it hostage. I gave a name of a very good friend with whom I agreed that he will be the hostage, Dr. Edward Packman. A lawyer who was active in Zionist roles, a fraternity brother of mine. He agreed to this. But I never gave his name to the police by some snafu, lack of cooperation between the SS and the police that issued the visas and passport. They never asked me. Of course I didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you meet in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went straight to the office of the revisionist Betar organization, to Mr. Jabotinsky. And I reported to him what I have done. And had before as a kind of-- which I did not even know and a kind of slogan. That one of his speeches said, that we should immigrate to Israel whether they want us or not. And he called it, it should become a national sport. But at the time, the immigration was smuggling via Syria to Turkey and Syria. Five for ten or four across the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had received orders. I had received a letter of recommendation from this Quaker to leading British. One of them was Mr.-- what's the name of this movie? Who is in-- who is in [INAUDIBLE]. Fletcher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg. Mr. Landsberg was the leader of the labor party. And I had a letter of invitation to Mr. Landsberg. I had a letter of invitation from the Quaker to [INAUDIBLE], who was a permanent Jewish member of Parliament. And to somebody else who I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the first time you had met Jabotinsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had met him several times before.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in England--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you able to accomplish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- these people helped me very much. I got there an appointment with the Colonial Minister, Mr. McDonald. They arranged for it. And I had a minister-- an appointment with Lord Winterton, who was the choice for the representation at a [INAUDIBLE] conference. I did not know that's at the conference in preparation. But I didn't achieve anything. Jabotinsky wrote to the Council of German Jewry. It was the official organization of the world organizations for the German refugees for support. And he got an-- that they are against, that your responsible steps. They didn't hold you responsible. They are against supporting any kind of immigration which is not properly organized. And for whose arrival not proper, preparations are being made.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got some money. I got 300 pounds form Mrs.-- she was a very known millionaire into Zionists. The owner of the Marx department stores. I don't know her name now. It's mentioned in my book. I got the 300 pound contribution, which is a big amount. It was a fortune. And the day afterwards, she called Jabotinsky. And told Jabotinsky she did not know that this is the revisionist undertaking and she withdraws her support. And I hadn't cashed the check yet. And Jabotinsky said, you have to return the check. And I said, I don't want to. But he was very much of a gentleman. He said, you got it under false pretenses. You have to return the check. I told you this. We can save many people because we have already the down payment for another ship. No, you have to return it. It was obtained under wrong pretense. And I returned the check.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you left England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in England and some other people were-- prepared the next transport. They went to Athens. They got money again, foreign currency. You know, this is, we said, foreign currency. It was foreign currency in Germany at this time was like diamonds because they needed all a foreign money in order to get more weapons. They were preparing for the war. But they had again got foreign currency. Had gone to England, to Athens, and had paid 2,000 pounds for a ship this was showing to them with the name of And then when we were back in Vienna, and trying to correspond, this belonged to other people. They had been the victims of swindlers. And it was what happens. They decided to leave anyhow. And they left for the border until the border on the Australian-Yugoslav border, where there were-- also Italian border. Where they had basically because they didn't have any Italian transit visa. The transit visa was given only for the shipping port for that they could really transit just-- there was no ships there. So I was punished in Vienna and there were things he told me. You go back. Well, I was not enthused about it. We met in Fiume. Two people from the Vienna transport who had a [INAUDIBLE] with one of them and somebody else that came. And we met in Fiume trying to get a visa from the Italians. Without a ship. For let's say, put in the meanwhile be out. And a group of about 50, I think, Betarim had at the same time arrived from the Baltic countries to whom we owe this ship. And we had some hidden in the basement of a synagogue. And the ship didn't come. The ship didn't come. And one day, I had a friend in Athens who had connections there. And one day in the morning-- it was in the early morning, I remember-- there was a man calling for me. Elegant, a very good looking man. My wife says that he was the best-looking man she ever saw. Very impressive looking man who told me that he is representing the devised group, which was a little lodge underground group. They had smuggled liquor into Germany, into the United States as long as the Prohibition was on, and now they were without work. And he said, if you do the-- I said, but we don't have any money. He says, you have 2,000 pounds. I said, I've given them there. He said, you will never get them back from me [INAUDIBLE]. But at [INAUDIBLE] we'll get them back. We take, I think his said, as account payment. I don't know whether I suggested it or that it was his idea. Anyhow, he accepted a down payment of 2,000 pounds, and immediately a ship was sent which was then in a port in [INAUDIBLE]. Was sent to Fiume. That was the most [NON-ENGLISH]. Draga was supposed to arrive the next morning. And in the evening I heard that Eichmann had decided not to radio. We promised him all the time, tomorrow, in two days, and so on. There had been about two weeks in this [INAUDIBLE], which was very bad. Because for the sudden diarrhea, they had to use the toilets. And this collected on the bottom of the wagons. Or then they were permitted to go out but under another guard. And the guards took Jews to relieve themselves outside. But there was no food or nothing. They came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he ordered them back, I called and talked. Eichmann said, when the ships [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I said, if the ship is not there by 5:00 in the afternoon-- that was when it came the next morning-- they all go back to Dachau. Then I told him, I think the ship will be here. But if you go to Dachau, you know that here these are disciplined people. They are under orders to be ready to fulfilll to repeat what people did at the-- what is the rock? Masada. They will commit suicide, and you will have an international scandal on our hands, which at this point, Germany doesn't want, I'm sure. That you do not want, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very furious. He hung up. But this didn't go back to Dachau. They arrived at the railroad station and sent home. It was-- parents were horrified. As soon as they left, they showed they were already in Palestine. And their children came back. They were again now probably more than before. But they stayed there. And I had arranged for a ship to go. And but the ship left them. They couldn't reorganize it so fast in Germany. The ship left 3/4 empty. I took the 50 people in who had come from the border countries. I've already bring some out and show that we did not dare the undertaking. And then the word had gotten around in southern France and in Italy that you can get illegally to Palestine. There are people assembled in the ports. We took them all up without money and Draga left with about 150. I don't know how many. It was very few people for Palestine. And landed without any problem. We landed on Yom Kippur, on Erev Yom Kippur. And this is another 120 or 150. I do not know the number. In my book is the number that are saved. And Draga had orders immediately to return now to Galatz. Because we had arranged in the meanwhile, the people in Vienna had arranged, for transit visas through Romania. And Jabotinsky had some connection with Romania. This is through Romania. Then they could then leave via Galatz to-- but they didn't-- It comes to my mind they didn't have the transit visas through Romania. This man, Dr. Diamand, was a member of the Revisionist Party, a scientist. He told us, if you have trouble with transit visas, you don't need transit visas. They can all go on the Danube. The Danube is by international agreement. I have to interrupt you because we're out of tape. Yeah. Just one moment.
